petitioner_background: The petitioner is a painter from Nepal seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the arts under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act. He submitted evidence of exhibitions in Nepal and abroad (Japan and the United States), newspaper articles, certificates of prizes from various exhibitions, seven letters of recommendation, and an unpublished portfolio of his paintings. Many of the documents were in a foreign language and accompanied only by uncertified English translations. The petitioner is described in recommendation letters as a young artist with future potential working to promote Nepalese art.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Certificates and other evidence of 12 prizes or awards for painting in Nepal, nine of which were documented by materials in a foreign language with uncertified English translations. One English-language certificate for 'Bicycle Rally '89' from over 14 years before the petition was filed. Three exhibition brochures in English: (1) '27th National Art and Craft Exhibition (2053)' where the petitioner received a 'Contemporary Art Consolation Prize,' with a statement by Uttam Nepali of the Royal Nepal Academy Faculty of Fine Arts and Crafts; (2) '30th National Art and Craft Exhibition 2056' organized by the Nepal Association of Fine Art (NAFA) at the National Birendra Art Gallery, where he won first prize in the Traditional Arts category, with an introductory essay by Chairman Vijay Thapa; (3) 'Paintings Exhibition' organized by the Commercial Artists' Association of Nepal (CAAN) held at the Art Council Babarmahal in Kathmandu on August 17, 2001, where he received first prize, with introduction by CAAN Central President I.B. Malla noting approximately 15,000 members.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that without certified translations of the nine foreign-language documents, it could not determine whether the evidence supported the petitioner's claim per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). The Bicycle Rally '89 certificate had no documentation showing its relation to his work as an artist. For the 27th National Exhibition, Uttam Nepali's statement did not explain the significance of the consolation prize. For the 30th National Exhibition, Vijay Thapa only commented on the difficulty of selection. The dates of both National exhibitions were in a non-Gregorian calendar without explanation or translation. The record contained no critical reviews or national media coverage of the exhibitions and prize-winning works, and none of the recommendation letters discussed receipt of these awards. The record did not demonstrate that the NAFA and CAAN prizes were nationally recognized prizes reflecting sustained national or international acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: An 'Artist Identity Card' issued by the Artists' Society of Nepal identifying the petitioner as a 'Gen. Member' in the category of 'Con. Art,' valid to 'Sawan 2060.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The petitioner did not claim eligibility under this criterion on appeal. The record contained no evidence of the Society's membership criteria or any other evidence that outstanding achievements were prerequisite to Society membership. Additionally, without translation of the date into the Gregorian calendar, the AAO could not determine if the membership was consistent with sustained acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Six newspaper articles purportedly about the petitioner and his work, all printed in foreign language with uncertified English translations. On appeal, counsel contended three articles were published in leading national Nepalese periodicals and submitted additional evidence about the periodicals. Also submitted on appeal were three additional newspaper articles that reviewed a restaurant where the petitioner's painting of the goddess Kumari appeared in the background of photographs.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: Without certified translations of the foreign-language documents, the AAO could not determine whether the articles supported the petitioner's claim per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). The three restaurant review articles did not meet the criterion because they were not about the petitioner or his work, and they were published after the petition was filed. The petitioner must establish eligibility at the time of filing per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Seven letters of recommendation, two with uncertified English translations. Five letters from individuals involved with fine arts in Nepal: (1) Mr. Mishra, another Nepalese artist, describing petitioner as 'highly energetic, serious to discipline' and hoping he would become well known; (2) Artistic Director of Aarohan Theatre Group describing the petitioner's teaching of street children through an Aarohan-UNICEF partnership from 1994-1995, and noting he designed posters for five Aarohan productions; (3) A Nepalese artist praising petitioner's efforts to conserve and promote Nepalese arts through self-study and describing him as 'energetic to let understand the modern Nepalese art'; (4) A representative of the Nepal Artists' Society describing 'marvelous compositions'; (5) A representative of NAFA stating he has woven 'himself to be a milestone in the world of young artists'; and a letter from Durga Gallery noting his efforts to promote Nepalese art internationally. Counsel also cited invitations to display work in India, Japan and the United States.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted that while the statute does not require international recognition, the record did not demonstrate the petitioner was recognized for making original artistic contributions of major significance in Nepal consistent with sustained national acclaim. The two letters with uncertified translations could not be considered. Letters solicited in support of an immigration petition carry less weight than preexisting, independent evidence. Mr. Mishra did not discuss any specific major contributions. The Aarohan teaching letter attested to commendable teaching but did not establish an original major contribution, and the record lacked evidence that the posters were recognized for original and major contribution. The letters discussed promotion of Nepalese art generally but did not specifically describe how he made original contributions of major significance. Most letters described him as a young artist and discussed future potential rather than past accomplishments. The record contained no evidence that he significantly influenced other visual artists in Nepal or abroad or had a major impact on his field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Eight exhibitions of the petitioner's work in Nepal between 1995 and 2001, one solo exhibition at the Japanese Tea House in Tokyo in 2000, and one solo show at the Artists' Museum in Washington, DC in 2002 (November 2-29, 2002). Programs and postcards from exhibitions in Nepal and Japan, including an exhibition brochure with introduction praising the petitioner's work. A 'Rental Exhibition Contract' with the Artists' Museum showing total cost of $2,100. An undated letter from Rajendra Oli, President of the America Nepal Society, explaining the DC exhibition was part of the petitioner's travel plan, and stating the Royal Nepal Ambassador would inaugurate the exhibition on November 17, 2002. An October 31, 2002 letter from the Royal Nepalese Embassy. A printout from the museum's website. An article from Spacetime with uncertified translation misstating the exhibition date. A Lalit Festival program with foreword by a Co-chairman stating 47 leading artists from Nepal joined the exhibition.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that frequent display of one's artwork is inherent to the petitioner's profession, and duties or activities nominally falling under a regulatory criterion do not demonstrate national or international acclaim if they are inherent or routine in the occupation itself. Apart from programs and postcards, the record contained no evidence of the exhibitions or their significance. The Japanese Tea House exhibition had no evidence of critical acclaim, media coverage, or that the venue was prestigious. The Artists' Museum in DC was a rental exhibition gallery personally paid for by the petitioner ($2,100), and an exhibition procured through payment by the artist does not demonstrate requisite sustained acclaim; the only stated selection criterion was prior review and acceptance. The Royal Nepalese Embassy letter did not confirm that the Ambassador would inaugurate the exhibition. No evidence confirmed the petitioner exhibited at the Nepalese Embassy. The Lalit Festival foreword did not state that artists were invited or that exhibition was by invitation only. Additional evidence of another cultural festival display could not be considered because it arose after the petition was filed.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: No evidence initially submitted. On appeal, counsel claimed satisfaction and submitted evidence of sales of the petitioner's artwork.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO could not consider this evidence because it arose after the petition was filed; a petition cannot be approved at a future date after the petitioner becomes eligible under a new set of facts per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, finding that the petitioner failed to establish sustained national or international acclaim as an artist placing him at the very top of his field at the time of filing. The majority of documents submitted were in a foreign language with uncertified translations, making them non-probative per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). Much evidence on appeal arose after the petition was filed and could not be considered under Matter of Katigbak. The petitioner failed to meet any of the regulatory criteria discussed, and the burden of proof remained with the petitioner under Section 291 of the Act.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Significant procedural issue: the majority of documents submitted with the petition were in a foreign language accompanied only by uncertified English translations, violating 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), which requires that foreign language documents be accompanied by a full English translation certified as complete and accurate by a competent translator. As a result, much of the evidence was deemed non-probative and accorded no weight. Additionally, much evidence submitted on appeal arose after the petition was filed and could not be considered under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak.
